title: ExoMol line lists -- LXIII: ExoMol line lists for 12 isotopologues of CO2
journal: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
abstract: Extensive rovibrational line lists are constructed for 12 isotopologues of carbon dioxide: 2 6 , 3 6 , 2 7 , 3 7 , 2 8 , 3 8 , 6 2 7 , 6 2 8 , 6 3 7 , 6 3 8 , 7 2 8 , and 7 3 8 . The variational program TROVE was employed together with an exact kinetic energy operator, accurate empirical potential energy surface (Ames-2) and the ab initio dipole moment surface Ames-2021-40K. Empirical energy levels from the most recent MARVEL analyses, as well as from the HITRAN and CDSD databases, are used to replace calculated values where available. The line lists are further supplemented by assigning AFGL quantum numbers using machine-learning based estimators. The resulting data were employed to generate opacities with four radiative transfer codes, TauREx, ARCiS, NEMESIS, and petitRADTRANS, both for individual isotopologues and for CO2 at terrestrial isotopic natural abundance. All line lists and associated data are available at www.exomol.com.
body_text:
Introduction

Carbon dioxide (CO2) is a key atmospheric constituent across a wide range of planetary environments, including Earth , Venus , and Mars , as well as exoplanets and brown dwarfs . In the Earth's atmosphere, CO2 is the second most abundant greenhouse gas after water vapour , and is therefore central to studies in environmental chemistry. Several dedicated missions monitor Earth's atmospheric CO2 content. Since CO2 is a major component of the atmospheres of Earth, Mars, and Venus, remote sensing of this molecule is expected to be equally important for characterising exoplanets with similar properties. For example, CO2 dominates the opacity of the Venusian atmosphere, comprising approximately 95\%\ of its composition .
The critical role of CO\2\ in atmospheric retrievals has been highlighted in multiple planetary science studies ranging from terrestrial remote sensing (e.g. ) to planetary atmospheres such as Mars (e.g., ).

In the context of exoplanetary science, CO2 is anticipated to be a key molecular absorber across a broad range of planetary types, from temperate terrestrial planets to hot Jupiters. Its strong 4.3 m band has been unambiguously detected with JWST in the atmospheres of WASP-39 b , WASP-166 b , and the sub-Neptune K2-18 b . Consequently, comprehensive and accurate opacity data for CO2 and its isotopologues, valid over wide spectral and temperature ranges, are essential for probing atmospheric composition and physical conditions such as temperature and pressure. Such data underpin high-accuracy atmospheric models and are crucial for interpreting spectroscopic observations from ground-based telescopes and space missions, including JWST and the future ESA space observatory Ariel .

Detection of CO2 with high-resolution spectroscopy has not yet been achieved, mainly due to challenges associated with telluric contamination , although searches are ongoing . Isotopic ratios, in particular 3 6 /2 6 , have been proposed as tracers of carbon isotope fractionation in exoplanetary atmospheres, with potential links to biological processes detectable by JWST . Recently, four isotopologues of CO2 - 2 6 , 3 6 , 6 2 8 , and 6 2 7 - were detected in a terrestrial planet-forming region of an externally irradiated Herbig disk using VLT .

Significant progress in developing high-resolution, variationally computed CO2 line lists has been made in recent years by the NASA Ames group and the ExoMol project . High-temperature line lists are particularly important for modelling exoplanetary atmospheres, and numerous works have targeted this goal . Recent high-temperature datasets include UCL-4000 , Ames-2021 , the latest Ames line lists AI-3000K and its HITEMP compilation . An accurate room-temperature line list CDSD-2024-PI was recently reported by . While ExoMol and Ames line lists combine experimental results with variational calculations, improved with empirically-derived energies, the CDSD-2024-PI and HITRAN line lists rely on effective Hamiltonian models. All provide accurate and comprehensive rovibrational data for CO2, but further improvements in accuracy and completeness remain necessary.

An additional aspect relevant to this work is the treatment of pressure broadening. A large body of literature exists on CO2 broadening coefficients with different perturbing species; here we only highlight a few recent developments. presented a detailed analysis of a large set of experimental broadening parameters for the main isotopologue colliding with N2, O2, and CO2, with further analyses and reassessments reported by and .

In this work we present extensive rovibrational line lists for 12 isotopologues of CO2: 2 6 , 3 6 , 2 7 , 3 7 , 2 8 , 3 8 , 6 2 7 , 6 2 8 , 6 3 7 , 6 3 8 , 7 2 8 , and 7 3 8 . The line lists were generated with \ using an exact kinetic energy operator (KEO), the empirical potential energy surface (PES) Ames-2 and the \ dipole moment surface (DMS) Ames-2021-40K . To improve line position accuracy, (i) we employed the empirical band-centre correction approach , shifting the diagonal vibrational Hamiltonian terms to match experimental values, followed by (ii) substitution of calculated energy levels with empirical ones from \ studies , HITRAN , and CDSD-2024-PI . As part of this work, empirical energies of the minor CO2 isotopologues were extracted from HITRAN using the \ procedure.

The resulting line lists, in ExoMol format , contain energies, Einstein A coefficients, lifetimes, uncertainties, and quantum numbers for 12 isotopologues of CO2. Partition functions and broadening parameters are also provided. Associated opacities were computed for the four radiative transfer codes ARCiS , TauREx , NEMESIS and petitRADTRANS using the ExoMolOP procedure , both for individual isotopologues and for a composite opacity assuming terrestrial isotopic abundances. All data are available from the ExoMol database at www.exomol.com.

Rovibrational states of CO2 isotopologues: quantum numbers and selection rules

Accurate description of the rovibrational states is essential for the construction and assignment of spectroscopic line lists. Quantum numbers provide the link between the variationally computed eigenstates of CO2 and the spectroscopic notation used in experimental and atmospheric studies. For CO2 and its isotopologues, several labelling schemes exist, reflecting both the linear triatomic structure of the molecule and the strong Fermi-resonance interactions that complicate vibrational assignments. In addition, nuclear-spin statistics and molecular symmetry play a central role in determining the allowed rovibrational levels and transition selection rules. In the following subsections we outline the quantum number conventions, symmetry classifications, and selection rules adopted in this work, following both spectroscopic practice and the requirements of large-scale automatic assignments.

Vibrational states of symmetric (non-linear) XY2 molecules can be described by three normal modes, conventionally denoted as (symmetric stretch), (bend), and (asymmetric stretch). For linear symmetric triatomic molecules such as CO2, Herzberg's notation is commonly used to label vibrational states. The three modes are associated with vibrational quantum numbers , , and . The superscript denotes the vibrational angular momentum associated with the doubly-degenerate bending mode in the linear configuration. This quantum number represents the projection of the vibrational angular momentum along the molecular axis and takes values from to in steps of two. In spectroscopic notation, however, the absolute value is often used.

The most widely adopted scheme for labelling vibrational states of CO2 isotopologues is that of the Air Force Geophysics Laboratory (AFGL) . The AFGL notation employs a quintuplet of quantum numbers designed to simplify the description of strong Fermi resonances in CO2 of states and , including their overtones and combination states, i.e. between states of the type and .
The vibrational quantum numbers ( , , ) are identical for all members of the same resonance polyad, while the fifth index ranks the states within it. In AFGL notation, and are always equal, meaning . By convention, for a given Fermi multiplet, starts from 1 and increases by removing one quantum of and adding two quanta of .

For example, the Fermi-resonant states , , and correspond to , , and , respectively . The polyad number is approximately conserved within a Fermi multiplet.

For asymmetric isotopologues such as 6 2 7 , the AFGL quantum numbers follow the same scheme, , even though the concepts of symmetric and asymmetric stretching lose their strict meaning. While AFGL notation provides a useful framework for analysing resonance clusters, it is not well suited for the automatic assignment of very large datasets.

Rotational quantum number and parity

The total angular momentum of CO2 is denoted by , which includes contributions from both overall molecular rotation and vibrational angular momentum ( ).

The Pauli principle imposes restrictions on symmetric isotopologues containing zero-spin oxygen nuclei, namely C16O2 and C18O2. For these species, vibrational states with even (symmetric under oxygen exchange) are only associated with even , while states with odd (antisymmetric) only allow odd . This reflects the requirement that the total rovibrational wavefunction be symmetric under exchange of the two oxygen nuclei. The rotationless parity of a state is labelled or and is determined by the sum : states with even values correspond to parity, and those with odd values to parity.

For C17O2 (2 7 and 3 8 ), the 17O nuclei have non-zero spin ( ). In this case the Pauli principle does not restrict odd or even levels, and both ortho and para nuclear-spin symmetries exist. All values are allowed, and bands with exhibit -type doubling, giving rise to both and parity levels for each .

Symmetry group classification

An alternative classification of rovibrational states uses molecular symmetry groups , specifically C2v(M) or Cs(M), and their irreducible representations (irreps). For symmetric CO2, states transform according to the C2v(M) group, which has four irreps: , , , and .

For isotopologues with zero-spin oxygen nuclei, the Pauli principle forbids rovibrational states of and symmetry, leaving only and , which correlate with parity according to:
 

For symmetric CO2 with non-zero oxygen nuclear spin, all four irreps ( , , , ) are allowed. These can be correlated with ortho and para spin symmetries as summarised in Table . For the labels, the convention for and for is applied, where denotes molecular parity.

For asymmetric isotopologues, the rovibrational states transform as or in the Cs(M) group. Their relation to parity follows the same rules as in the symmetric, zero-spin case:
 

Selection rules

Rotational selection rules for linear molecules are . For CO2, Q-branch transitions ( ) occur only in parallel bands and involve transitions, where the dipole moment change is parallel to the molecular axis. P- and R-branch transitions ( ) occur in both parallel and perpendicular bands and involve or .

For symmetric isotopologues with non-zero oxygen nuclear spin, additional nuclear-spin selection rules apply:
 

In terms of molecular symmetry, these correspond to
 

For asymmetric isotopologues, the selection rule is general for all dipole allowed transitions and therefore still holds; in group-theoretical notation it is expressed as
 

The nuclear-spin degeneracy factors for the isotopologues considered in this work are summarised in Table .

 

Line list production

The generation of high-quality line lists requires three key ingredients:
(i) accurate PES and DMS to describe molecular structure and transition intensities;
(ii) robust nuclear-motion calculations to solve the rovibrational Schr\" o dinger equation;
and (iii) empirical refinement procedures to bring calculated energy levels into agreement with experimental data.
In this section we outline the methodology adopted for CO2 isotopologues, including the choice of potential and dipole moment surfaces, the details of the variational calculations, and the empirical corrections applied to improve the accuracy of the final line lists.

Potential Energy Surface

We initially considered using the more recent empirical PES Ames-X01d of CO2 developed by , which was optimised to treat all isotopologues on an equal footing. The earlier PES, Ames-2 , originally developed for the main isotopologue 2 6 , was also available and had previously been used to construct the UCL-4000 line list . However, out tests suggested that the optimisation in Ames-X01d came at the cost of somewhat degraded line list quality.
For 2 6 and 3 8 , this is illustrated in Fig. , which compares -calculated term values with experimentally derived values (MARVEL or CDSD-2024-PI ) using both PESs. Ames-2 provides a more compact and flatter -dependence, indicating more accurate equilibrium values and greater suitability for subsequent band-centre corrections. Our tests confirmed this trend across all 12 isotopologues, with Ames-2 consistently giving better agreement with \ and HITRAN energies, at least using . We therefore adopted Ames-2 for all line lists reported in this work.

Dipole Moment Surface

For transition intensities we used the ab initio DMS Ames-2021-40K of CO2 by , the latest in the Ames series of dipole moment surfaces. In our previous line list, UCL-4000, the DMS of 
 was employed.
 

Variational nuclear-motion calculations

The rovibrational Schr\" o dinger equation was solved using the program \ . Our methodology follows and , employing the exact KEO in the bisector frame together with basis functions constructed from associated Laguerre polynomials. For full details the reader is referred to these works; here we summarise only the main steps.

First, vibrational eigenfunctions were obtained variationally for by diagonalising the Hamiltonian in all relevant irreducible representations ( , , , or , for symmetric or asymmetric species, respectively). The primitive basis was constructed as a product of 1D vibrational functions , , and , where and are stretching coordinates and with the bond angle .

For , rovibrational basis functions were formed as contracted, symmetrised products of the vibrational functions and rigid-rotor functions:
 
where are symmetrised rigid-rotor functions , is constrained by ( ), and , , are the total, vibrational, and rotational symmetries in C2v(M) or Cs(M). Nuclear masses adopted were: Da (12C) and 13.000063355 Da (13C); Da (16O), 16.99474312 Da (17O), and 17.99477097 Da (18O).

Rovibrational wavefunctions were computed up to (see Table ) and used to generate transition intensities for all dipole-allowed transitions. For 2 6 , lower and upper state energies were limited to 16\,000 cm-1 and 36\,000 cm-1, respectively. For 3 6 , the lower-state energy cut-off was set to 12 000 cm-1. For all other isotopologues the lower-state cut-off was 10\,000 cm-1. Transition wavenumbers spanned 0-20\,000 cm-1. Nuclear spin statistical weights for all isotopologues are given in Table .

Empirical corrections of energies

To improve calculated rovibrational energies, empirical band-centre corrections (EBCC) were applied to the diagonal elements of the Hamiltonian matrices. These corrections were obtained as average residuals between -calculated band origins and empirical values (from , CDSD-2024-PI, or HITRAN2020). For 2 6 , the procedure is illustrated in Fig. .

Since basis functions are eigenfunctions of the vibrational Hamiltonian, their diagonal elements can be straightforwardly replaced by empirical energies, thereby propagating improvements to higher- rovibrational levels . These also include states with , which formally do not exist in Nature but are present in the basis set.
This significantly reduced obs.-calc. residuals across most isotopologues. The exception was 6 2 8 , where some bands diverged at high after EBCC (Fig. , green crosses). For this isotopologue, band-centre shifts were not applied.

MARVELisation procedure

Further improvements were made using the hybrid MARVELisation approach , in which calculated energies are replaced, where available, by more accurate values derived from \ or effective Hamiltonian (EH) analyses.
 \ energies were taken from the recent \ studies of CO2 isotopologues , while effective Hamiltonian energies were taken from CDSD-2024-PI (2 6 ) and HITRAN2020 (all minor isotopologues). The EH energies are used to minimise gaps in available \ energy levels.

To this end, the EH energies were generated using the \ algorithm applied to HITRAN transitions . The HITRAN uncertainness were set based on the corresponding HITRAN error code , for example, for code 4 (0.0001 \ 0.001 ), the uncertainty of 0.0005 \ was assumed.
For the main isotopologue 2 6 , the CDSD-2024-PI energies were readily provided by . The \ procedure ensures self-consistency of the derived network, with uncertainties propagated through the bootstrap algorithm . While \ is usually applied to directly measured transitions, here it is used on effective Hamiltonian-derived frequencies to supplement the empirical dataset.

In the ExoMol states files, purely -derived levels are labelled "Ma", HITRAN-derived levels as "HI", and effective Hamiltonian values as "EH" (see the description of the hybrid MARVELisation approach of ). For the main isotopologue 2 6 , \ data from were supplemented by CDSD-2024-PI . The HITRAN energies generated using the \ approach are provided as part of the supplementary material.

Table also compares the number of lines for each isotopologue with a transition intensity greater
than cm/molecule at 296 K. For some reason which remains unclear, given that our computed line lists are
complete, HITRAN contains slightly more lines for each isotopologue.

 

 

 

Rovibrational line lists for CO2 isotopologues

Using the \ rovibrational wavefunctions and the ab initio DMS Ames-2021-40K, we generated 12 line lists for CO2 isotopologues, collectively referred to as Dozen, in the ExoMol format . The coverage extends from 0 to 20\,000 cm-1 (i.e. wavelengths m). For the parent and most important isotopologue, 2 6 , we adopted a more extensive coverage of state excitations: a higher lower-state energy cut-off of 16\,000 cm-1 (cf. 10\,000-12\,000 cm-1 for other isotopologues). A larger increases computational cost but improves population coverage of excited states and thus yields a larger, more complete line list (see Table ). Further control of the excitation coverage is provided by and , which were selected per isotopologue (Table ). The choice of affects the size of the basis set and thus its quality.

Each line list comprises a .states file, a set of .trans files, and a partition-function file .pf. As an example, an extract from the 2 6 .states file is given in Table . It contains state term values , total degeneracies , uncertainties (unc), lifetimes, and quantum numbers. Vibrational labels are provided in three conventions: Herzberg ( , , ), AFGL ( , , , , ), and \ local-mode indices ( , , , ). Both rotationless parity ( ) and molecular symmetry ( , , , , , ) are provided. States are indexed by an integer (state ID), used in the transition files.

The .states files are MARVELised: where available, calculated term values are replaced by empirical values, \ (Ma), HITRAN (HI) or effective-Hamiltonian (CDSD; EH), with the label supplied in the penultimate column; the original calculated value is retained in the final column. State-dependent lifetimes computed with Dozen\ are included (column 6; see Table ).

For practicality, each isotopologue's transition set is divided into 20 .trans files, each covering a 1000 cm-1 bin over 0-20\,000 cm-1. An extract from a 2 6 .trans file is shown in Table , listing Einstein A coefficients and the upper/lower state IDs.

Using machine learning to reconstruct spectroscopic quantum numbers

In order to help with the usage of the Dozen line list, quantum numbers for all three assignment schemes are provided as part of the States file. While the \ quantum numbers are generated using the largest contribution to the associated eigenfunctions, to assign vibrational quantum numbers for AFGL and Herzberg they are not known, we developed a machine learning (ML) pipeline. To this end, a classifier was implemented in Pytorch using a multi-head neural network architecture containing a shared backbone and specialized output heads.

The ML model starts with a shared feature extraction network consisting of 6 fully connected layers (512 256 128 128 64 neurons) with GELU activations and dropout regularization to learn common representations from 32 molecular features (energy, quantum numbers, dipole moments, isotopic masses, symmetries). From the 64-dimensional shared representation, 8 separate classification heads branch out, each containing 3 additional layers (64 32 output classes) to predict the specific quantum numbers (Herzberg: , , , and and AFGL: , , , and ). This design enables the backbone to capture shared molecular physics, while each head specializes in its respective quantum-number assignment task. Crucially, we enforce quantum-mechanical constraints within the loss function, limiting values according to the parity of .

We assess model uncertainty using Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout , which keeps dropout layers active during inference by effectively sampling multiple network realizations. We perform 50 forward passes per sample, obtaining a distribution of class probabilities. From the mean probability vector , we compute predictive entropy: , normalized to by dividing by . A normalized entropy threshold of 0.4, determined from prior validation to optimize the trade-off between accepted correct predictions and rejected incorrect ones, was applied. Predictions below this threshold were accepted, enabling the assignment of over one million spectral lines across all targeted isotopologues.

For different isotopologues, the ML procedure resulted in 80 000-120 000 states additionally assignments with AFGL and Herzberg. While this represents only 1.4-3\% of the total number of states in each data set, this is a multifold increase comparing to the empirically assignment states, which is illustrated in Fig. .

 

Partition functions

 

Partition functions were computed for all 12 isotopologues on a 1 K grid from 0 to 5000 K using the direct sum
 
where is the nuclear-spin statistical factor (Table ), is the rotational quantum number, is the second radiation constant (cm\,K), and is the energy term value (cm-1) relative to the ground state ( , , , ).

Figure compares the Dozen\ partition function for 2 6 with TIPS 2024 (total internal partition sum) of HITRAN2020 and with that obtained from the Ames AI-3000K line list . The Dozen\ and Ames curves agree closely and both lie systematically above the HITRAN TIPS values, reflecting improved completeness of the underlying states; the lack of convergence of the HITRAN TIPS partition function at high temperature has been noted previously .

 

 

Opacities

Temperature- and pressure-dependent opacities for all 12 isotopologues of CO2, based on the Dozen\ line lists, were generated using the ExoMolOP procedure . Opacity tables were produced for four widely used atmospheric retrieval codes: ARCiS , TauREx , NEMESIS , and petitRADTRANS , and are provided alongside the line lists. In addition, we supply combined opacities constructed for a representative atmosphere containing multiple isotopologues in terrestrial abundance ratios (see Table ).

Line broadening coefficients

Accurate treatment of collisional line broadening is essential for radiative-transfer modelling. We adopt a semi-empirical approach, combining theoretical predictions with carefully selected experimental data, to provide broadening and shifting coefficients for CO2 transitions with common perturbers (N2, O2, CO2, H2, He, and H2O) over the temperature range 200-2000 K.

Previous analyses have highlighted inconsistencies among experimental datasets. For example, and showed that different experiments on the same transition often disagree beyond the quoted uncertainties, complicating attempts to assess vibrational or isotopologue dependences of the half-width coefficients . More recent work by re-analysed the most reliable data, proposing a new set of semi-empirical parameters for CO2- and air-broadening, suitable for Voigt and speed-dependent Voigt profiles, and found no significant vibrational dependence of .

Isotopologue effects are also small. For example, found no statistically significant difference between N2-broadening of 6 3 8 and that of the main isotopologue, while estimated that isotopic mass effects reduce by less than 1\%. This was recently confirmed by cavity ringdown measurements of 3 6 by , who found air-broadening coefficients only 0.4\% smaller than those of 2 6 .

Given the very weak vibrational and isotopologue dependences, we provide only -dependent broadening coefficients and temperature exponents for the single power-law parametrisation. Speed dependence and line-mixing are not considered. The adopted and values are taken from HITRAN for broadening by H2, He , N2, CO2 , and H2O , and are supplied in the m0-diet format for use with ExoCross . In addition, we provide more recent theoretical water-broadening data in the m2-diet format , which employs a double power-law to describe the temperature dependence of more accurately.

Illustrations and analysis

In this section we demonstrate the performance of the new line lists by comparing their predicted spectra with previous datasets and with available experimental information. A general overview is provided in Fig. , which shows spectra of all isotopologues at K on a logarithmic scale, without scaling to isotopic abundances.

 

Figure illustrates the coverage and quality of the Dozen\ 2 6 line list at K, compared with our previous UCL-4000 list and the Ames AI-3000K list . The Dozen\ results are in excellent agreement with AI-3000K. We also compared with HITEMP 2025 , which was generated from AI-3000K using the super-lines and super-energies technique . As expected, the HITEMP spectrum follows AI-3000K closely over 0-12\,000 cm-1, which is the range adopted by HITEMP for 2 6 .

 

Compared to UCL-4000, the new line list exhibits more physical behaviour at higher wavenumbers. This was achieved by optimising the stretching basis set comparing to that used in by extending the
bond-length grid used in the numerical integrations from 2.2 \ to 2.6 . The wavenumber behaviour of Dozen\ is now
consistent with the near-integrated dipole limit (NIDL) , which predicts that overtone intensities decrease approximately exponentially, appearing as straight lines in a log-intensity plot . By contrast, the formation of plateaux at high overtones is a well-known indicator of numerical artefacts, as seen in UCL-4000 above 14\,000 cm-1. 

A more subtle intensity problem in UCL-4000 was identified by . Transitions in the 700 nm region ( 14\,300 cm-1) were predicted with intensities above cm/molecule, yet an extensive experimental search found no evidence for them. This band was therefore significantly overestimated in UCL-4000. The present line list, based on the improved basis set and different Ames-2021-40K DMS, brings these intensities below the 10-30 cm molecule-1 threshold value, as shown in Fig. .

 

Differences among isotopologues are illustrated in Fig. , which shows K spectra scaled by natural terrestrial abundances. Apart from the dominant 2 6 , only 3 6 and 6 2 8 produce IR features of sufficient strength to be detectable under atmospheric conditions. Of course,
this situation will change with the altered isotope abundances encountered elsewhere in space.

 

Finally, Figures and present stick spectra at K for all 12 isotopologues of CO2, separated into symmetric and asymmetric species. MARVELised transitions are highlighted, illustrating the extent of experimental coverage. Comparisons with HITRAN2020 (and CDSD-2024 for 2 6 ) further demonstrate the accuracy and completeness of the new line lists. A quantitative summary of line list statistics, including the number of empirically anchored transitions above the HITRAN intensity threshold of cm/molecule at K, is given in Table .

 

 

Conclusions

This work presents a new set of comprehensive rovibrational line lists for 12 isotopologues of CO2 collectively know as the Dozen\ line list. These line lists were computed using accurate empirical PESs and the latest ab initio DMSs from the Ames group , covering the spectroscopic range up to 20\,000 cm-1. The line list for the main isotopologue 2 6 is applicable up to at least 3000 K, while those for the minor isotopologues are reliable up to about 2000 K. The accuracy of the datasets has been enhanced through empirical band-centre corrections and systematic MARVELisation, incorporating experimental energies from , HITRAN, and CDSD where available.

The new ExoMol CO\2\ line lists represent a significant improvement over previous work. In particular, we now provide hot line lists for 12 isotopologues with an extended wavenumber coverage. Besides, spectra generated using Dozen\
display physically correct intensity behaviour for the high overtone bands thus avoiding the spurious plateau observed in UCL-4000 . The intensity anomaly in the 700 nm region is resolved by improving the stretching basis set used. Comparisons with HITRAN spectra at K show excellent agreement, confirming both the reliability of line positions and the accuracy of intensities.

For the first time, we employed machine-learning methods to reconstruct AFGL and `Herzberg' quantum numbers for CO2 isotopologues, significantly extending the quantum assignment coverage in a consistent and physically constrained manner. We are currently using machine-learning techniques to improve the prediction transition wavenumbers for isotopologues ; these results will be used to update Dozen states files for minor isotopologues with improved estimates for their energy levels in the near future. Partition functions have been computed on a fine temperature grid up to 5000 K, ensuring robust thermodynamic and opacity applications. Pressure-broadening coefficients with the most important perturbers (N2, O2, CO2, H2, He, H2O, and air) have been collected from the literature and are provided in ExoMol's diet formats (m0 and m2), enabling flexible use in radiative-transfer models.

Opacities were generated with ExoMolOP for four leading atmospheric retrieval codes (ARCiS, TauREx, NEMESIS, and petitRADTRANS). Combined opacity grids, scaled to terrestrial isotopic abundances, are also supplied for practical applications in planetary and stellar atmosphere modelling.

The line lists and associated data (partition functions, broadening parameters, opacities) are available in the ExoMol database at www.exomol.com. Together, they provide the most complete and accurate spectroscopic resource to date for CO2 and its isotopologues. These datasets will be invaluable for atmospheric retrievals across the Solar System, brown dwarfs, and exoplanets, as well as for laboratory and terrestrial remote-sensing studies. Future work will focus on extending the spectral range into the ultraviolet and further refining high-temperature coverage.
